text: For doing total laparoscopic hysterectomy in Kegel, a very huge uterus, it's important to put the trocar in the upper position. This trocar position allows us to have a better view on the entire uterus.

The surgeon begins the hysterectomy by releasing the lateral attachments of the uterus. The round ligament is cut, followed by the left fallopian tube. Bipolar forceps are used to coagulate the utero-ovarian ligament and control the left adnexal vein. After coagulation, the lateral attachments of the enlarged uterus are cut, resulting in a bloodless field.
The surgeon cuts the posterior leaf of the broad ligament to increase uterine mobility, carefully avoiding injury to the deep uterine vein visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs the same procedure on the opposite side, cutting the right round ligament and right tube using the harmonic scalpel. The right utero-ovarian ligament is controlled with bipolar coagulation before being cut with the harmonic, ensuring a bloodless procedure. After releasing both adnexa, the posterior leaf of the broad ligament is cut to increase uterine mobility, and dissection continues toward the upper part of the right uterosacral ligament.
If you follow these rules, there is absolutely no danger and no problem to manage very huge uterus, more than one kilogram.

The surgeon performs vesicovaginal dissection by cutting the peritoneum between the bladder and the isthmus. The Halban fascia is identified and incised, allowing the bladder to be pushed downward and exposing the anterior vaginal wall. The upper part of the right bladder pillar is cut, maintaining a bloodless field using either harmonic or classical devices like bipolar and scissors.

The surgeon skeletonizes the left uterine pedicle, exposing the artery and vein. Bipolar coagulation is applied directly to the vessel, allowing for safe cutting without bleeding. The dissection continues with the cutting of the posterior cervical vagina and vessel, followed by similar actions on the anterior side.

The surgeon uses a 10mm forceps to expose the right uterine pedicle. The pedicle is coagulated using bipolar coagulation, and the harmonic ace is used to cut the artery followed by the vein, which is medial to the artery. The entire right uterine pedicle is cut in a bloodless procedure.

The surgeon coagulates the uterine pedicle using bipolar electrocautery before cutting it with the harmonic ace, ensuring a bloodless procedure. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is then cut, extending towards the upper part of the uterosacral ligament.

The surgeon continues the dissection by cutting the posterior aspect of the vagina, progressively opening it. The cervical vaginal vessel on the right side is cut safely, preparing for an anterior corpotomy. The left uterine vein, initially preserved, is sectioned, facilitating a classical intrafascial hysterectomy.

The surgeon uses a monopolar needle to cut the anterior and left lateral aspects of the vagina, while the previously cut posterior vaginal valve is clearly visible. The right lateral aspect of the vagina is also cut, maintaining a bloodless field. The uterus appears enlarged, necessitating intra-abdominal morcellation.

The surgeon uses a Chardonnay knife, an endoscopic blade, inserted through a 10mm trocar positioned between the umbilicus and pubic line to cut the uterus. This technique facilitates the extraction of the uterus with a laparoscopic 10mm forceps, minimizing the risk of vaginal trauma and reducing vaginal manipulation.

The surgeon completes the uterine extraction and begins the vaginal closure using a Vicryl suture with a fourteen millimeter needle. The closure starts at the lateral aspect, with a posterior stitch placed in the upper part of the uterosacral ligament to reduce the risk of postoperative vaginal prolapse. The entire mucosa and peritoneum are included in the stitch to minimize postoperative bleeding.

The surgeon performs the vaginal closure using an extract of oil knotting technique, specifically a rudder knot, which is an autoblock knot. Interrupted sutures are used for better cicatrization, avoiding bipolar coagulation on the vaginal edges. The intracorporeal knotting technique is also employed to achieve a secure vaginal closure.
As you see, it's a simple technique. This is our rules for laparoscopic hysterectomy in cataracts, uterus. Thank you for attention.
